intro: The biggest real distinction between ThingsBoard and Medplum is straightforward: ThingsBoard is aimed at Teams that need self-hosted IoT with device mgmt, telemetry and rule chains, whereas Medplum targets Healthcare startups building EHR or patient-facing apps on FHIR with modern tooling. ThingsBoard sits at freemium with paid upgrades and carries low lock-in, while Medplum sits at freemium with paid upgrades and carries low lock-in. ThingsBoard is a fit when you need teams that need self-hosted iot with device mgmt, telemetry and rule chains — typical scenarios include open-source iot platform with device management and dashboards. Medplum is aimed at healthcare startups building ehr or patient-facing apps on fhir with modern tooling, with workloads like open-source fhir-native ehr backend for digital health teams as the common case. The honest trade-off: ThingsBoard is explicitly not for small teams wanting a polished managed iot saas with zero ops; Medplum is not intended for non-healthcare projects or teams that can't invest in learning fhir.
quick_take: ThingsBoard is for teams that need self-hosted iot with; Medplum is for healthcare startups building ehr or patient-facing; decide based on which workload you actually run.
verdict_a: Choose ThingsBoard if your primary workload is teams that need self-hosted iot with device mgmt, telemetry and rule chains and low switching cost matters.
verdict_b: Choose Medplum if your primary workload is healthcare startups building ehr or patient-facing apps on fhir with modern tooling and low switching cost matters.

faq.a: Neither ThingsBoard nor Medplum publishes a straightforward entry price in our data, so a direct 'cheaper' call is not possible. Pricing models differ (freemium vs freemium), which usually means the answer depends on seat count and usage.

faq.q: Can I migrate from ThingsBoard to Medplum?
faq.a: Migrating from ThingsBoard to Medplum is generally feasible because ThingsBoard has low lock-in, so data and configuration are usually portable. Since both tools sit in the same category (Backend-as-a-Service), the conceptual mapping is closer, but you still need to validate feature parity against your specific workload. Plan migration as a project, not a script: inventory integrations, test on non-production workloads, and keep ThingsBoard running in parallel until Medplum is verified.

Our data gives ThingsBoard and Medplum the same developer experience score (4/5), so neither has a clear edge on DX by that measure. The differentiator is usually how each tool fits your workflow — things like CLI quality, docs, local dev loop, and error messages. The safest call is to pilot both on a representative task and see which one gets out of your way.

faq.q: Is Medplum a good alternative to ThingsBoard?
faq.a: Medplum is in the same category as ThingsBoard (Backend-as-a-Service), so it's a reasonable alternative to evaluate. Medplum is aimed at healthcare startups building ehr or patient-facing apps on fhir with modern tooling, while ThingsBoard targets teams that need self-hosted iot with device mgmt, telemetry and rule chains — that difference should drive the switch decision. Note that Medplum is explicitly not for non-healthcare projects or teams that can't invest in learning fhir — if that describes your workload, it's not a viable replacement.
